messages.content: act as an an academic journal editor. please rephrase the paragraph form academic angle.

As the editor of an academic journal, I am responsible for the oversight and management of the publication process, ensuring that high-quality research articles are selected and published. I hold the crucial role of collaborating with researchers, reviewers, and the editorial board to ensure adherence to scholarly standards in terms of methodology, analysis, and content. My objective is to promote scholarly excellence, disseminate original research findings, and contribute to the advancement of knowledge in the specific field covered by the journal. Through rigorous peer review and constructive feedback, I aim to foster intellectual discourse and facilitate the dissemination of impactful research to a wider academic audience.

As observed in our probands, It is first definitively fetus abnormality of development case in reported. Unfortunately, Short stature and fused ribs did not detected by Ultrasound and MRI. The reason may be not detect by this method or the fetus do not develop the manifestations. DDl3(OMIM 602768) and DLL1(OMIM 606582) caused Spondylocostal dysostosis 1 and 7 , which do not also manifest the rib anomalies and vertebral fusion

In our study, we have encountered a unique case of fetal developmental abnormality that has not been previously reported in the literature. However, it is unfortunate that certain manifestations, such as short stature and fused ribs, were not detected through the use of ultrasound and MRI imaging techniques. There are two possible explanations for this: either these abnormalities were not detectable by the imaging methods employed, or the fetus did not develop these particular manifestations. It is worth noting that two genetic conditions, namely DDl3 (OMIM 602768) and DLL1 (OMIM 606582), have been associated with Spondylocostal dysostosis 1 and 7, respectively. However, it is important to highlight that these conditions do not typically exhibit rib anomalies or vertebral fusion as observed in this case.
So Ultrasound and MRI are difficulty to distinguish the subtype.

Distinguishing between different subtypes of the observed condition using ultrasound and MRI techniques poses considerable challenges. The limitations of these imaging methods may hinder the accurate identification and classification of specific subtypes within the broader spectrum of the condition. Consequently, it becomes crucial to explore alternative diagnostic approaches or additional investigative methods to enhance our ability to differentiate between distinct subtypes. This highlights the need for further research and advanced imaging modalities that can offer more precise characterization and classification of these subtypes.

To data, 17 LFNG variants have been reported in the literature(table2). Most of variants are missesne substitutions and affect he Fringe domain of the protein, which are responsible for the 
acetylglucosamine transferase activity. 5 variants are small deletion or duplication. c.822-5C>T are the only reported intronic variant, which impact on LFNG expression. Reported variants are almost evenly dispersed across all exon regions. There are not hot spots to be detected. The c.1078C>T variant are novel, and are the first reported in Chinese population and last exon in the LFNG.

To date, a total of 17 LFNG variants have been documented in the existing literature, as outlined in Table 2. The majority of these variants consist of missense substitutions, which specifically affect the Fringe domain of the LFNG protein. This domain is responsible for the crucial acetylglucosamine transferase activity. Among the reported variants, there are 5 instances of small deletions or duplications identified. Notably, a singular intronic variant, designated as c.822-5C>T, has been reported, and it has been shown to have an impact on LFNG expression. It is worth mentioning that the reported variants display an equitable distribution across all the exon regions, without any specific hotspots observed. Of particular interest, the variant c.1078C>T is novel, and it represents the first reported instance in the Chinese population. Additionally, this variant is located in the final exon of the LFNG gene.
Additionally, this variant is located in the final exon of the LFNG gene,  and no other variant was reported in the last exon

Furthermore, it is noteworthy that the c.1078C>T variant, which is novel, is situated exclusively in the last exon of the LFNG gene. No other variants affecting this particular exon have been reported in the literature. This highlights the unique nature of this variant and its specific impact on the final segment of the LFNG gene sequence. Further investigation is needed to determine the functional consequences and potential clinical significance of this variant in relation to LFNG-related conditions.

In addition, no other variants have been reported in the last exon of the LFNG gene, apart from this specific study.
In addition, no other variants have been reported in the last exon of the LFNG gene, apart from this specific study.

In addition to the findings in this specific study, no other variants have been documented within the final exon of the LFNG gene. This indicates that the reported variant, c.1078C>T, represents a unique alteration in this particular genomic region. Further investigations are necessary to fully comprehend the implications and potential significance of this variant in relation to LFNG-related disorders.

In conclusion, we reported 1 case of novel pathogenic variants in the LFNG gene. The variant in  GlcNAc-transferase activity was significantly reduced. We also summary the the existing literature, and reevaluated the pathogenicity of the variants by ACMG criteria. This study enriches our knowledge of the LFNG gene variant spectrum and clinical manifestation. It could help to guide that genetic counseling and procreation guidance.

In conclusion, this study presents a novel case of pathogenic variants identified in the LFNG gene. The observed variant resulted in a significant reduction in GlcNAc-transferase activity. Furthermore, a comprehensive review of the existing literature was conducted, and the pathogenicity of the variants was reevaluated using the ACMG criteria. This study contributes to our understanding of the spectrum of LFNG gene variants and their associated clinical manifestations. The findings have implications for genetic counseling and reproductive guidance, providing valuable information to guide clinical practice in managing individuals with LFNG-related conditions.
help me write a abstract using above information

The abstract below highlights the key findings and implications of the study based on the provided information:

Abstract:
This study presents an investigation into pathogenic variants in the LFNG gene, with a focus on their impact on GlcNAc-transferase activity. A novel case involving a unique variant was reported, demonstrating a significant reduction in the enzyme's activity. In addition, a systematic review of the existing literature was conducted, leading to a comprehensive reevaluation of variant pathogenicity using the ACMG criteria. These findings contribute to a deeper understanding of the range of LFNG gene variants and their associated clinical manifestations. Importantly, this knowledge can guide genetic counseling and procreation guidance, providing valuable information for clinical decision-making. By enriching our understanding of the LFNG gene variant spectrum, this study furthers clinical practice in managing individuals with LFNG-related conditions.

Abstract
Object  Spondylocostal dysostosis is a multiple gene causative a group of skeletal disorders, include vertebra and ribs multiple segmentation defects. This study was to analyze and identify pathogenic variants in the LFNG gene.
Methods  A case of Spondylocostal dysostosis patients was sequenced via whole exome sequencing. In vitro functional assays, Radiologic, Bioinformation analysis and systematic review were performed to confirm the etiology.
Result  18 variants have been reported as LFNG causal gene for SCDO3 by  a systematic review. We reported a Chinese SCDO3 case with vertebral dysplasia, scoliosis and tethered cord. The patient was a novel homozygous for c.1078C>T variants of LFNG, which encodes a glycosyltransferase. It’s loss of the enzyme function was confirmed by an in vitro enzyme assay. This is the first report of LFNG variant in Chinese and last exon.
Conclusion  In this study, a novel pathogenic variants in LFNG gene were described. Our research broaden the genotypic and phenotypic spectrum of LFNG gene in patient with Spondylocostal dysostosis.

Abstract:
Spondylocostal dysostosis encompasses a group of skeletal disorders characterized by multiple segmentation defects in the vertebrae and ribs, with a complex genetic etiology. This study aimed to analyze and identify pathogenic variants in the LFNG gene, implicated in Spondylocostal dysostosis. Whole exome sequencing was performed on a patient with Spondylocostal dysostosis, followed by in vitro functional assays, radiologic evaluation, bioinformatics analysis, and a systematic review to ascertain the etiology. The systematic review revealed 18 reported variants in LFNG associated with SCDO3. We reported a novel homozygous c.1078C>T variant in the LFNG gene in a Chinese patient, exhibiting vertebral dysplasia, scoliosis, and tethered cord. In vitro enzyme assays confirmed the loss of enzyme function caused by this variant. Notably, this is the first report of an LFNG variant in the Chinese population and within the last exon of the gene. This study describes a novel pathogenic LFNG variant and expands our understanding of the genotypic and phenotypic spectrum of LFNG gene involvement in patients with Spondylocostal dysostosis.
